improve_discovery: “Improve discovery” means enhancing how users find relevant content, products, or information on a platform. This involves optimizing search functionality, navigation structures, recommendation systems, and content targeting so users are more likely to encounter items that match their intent or interests. In a system like Chewy, this could include ranking algorithms, personalized content modules, and UI patterns that surface the right products at the right time, ultimately increasing engagement and conversion.
regression: “Regression” refers to a previously working feature or functionality breaking after new code changes are introduced. It is a common risk in software development, which is why automated testing and careful validation are used to ensure that updates do not unintentionally impact existing behavior.
continuous_integration_workflows: “Continuous integration workflows” are automated processes where code changes are frequently merged into a shared repository and automatically tested and validated. This helps catch bugs early, ensures code quality, and allows teams to ship updates more reliably and efficiently.
javascript_data_structures_and_algorithm: “JavaScript data structures and algorithms” refers to understanding how to organize and manipulate data efficiently using structures like arrays, objects, maps, and sets, along with implementing algorithms for searching, sorting, and problem-solving. This knowledge is critical for writing performant and scalable code.
debugging_technical_round: A “debugging technical round” is an interview format where candidates are asked to identify, analyze, and fix issues in existing code. It evaluates problem-solving ability, understanding of code behavior, and the ability to reason through bugs in a structured and efficient way.
basic_core_coding_fundamentals: “Basic core coding fundamentals” are the essential building blocks of programming, including variables, control flow (if/else, loops), functions, data structures, and basic problem-solving patterns. Mastery of these fundamentals is necessary for writing correct and maintainable code.
abstraction: “Abstraction” is the practice of simplifying complex systems by hiding unnecessary details and exposing only what is needed. It allows developers to focus on higher-level logic without needing to understand every underlying implementation detail.
docker_containers: “Docker containers and containers in general” refer to lightweight, portable units that package an application along with its dependencies so it can run consistently across different environments. Containers isolate applications and make deployment more predictable and efficient.
containerization: “Containerization” is the process of packaging applications and their dependencies into containers so they can run reliably across different environments. It improves consistency, scalability, and ease of deployment in modern software systems.
data_integrity: “Data integrity” refers to the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of data throughout its lifecycle. It ensures that data is not corrupted, lost, or improperly modified, which is critical for maintaining trust and correctness in systems.
devops: “DevOps” is a set of practices that combines software development and IT operations to improve collaboration, automate processes, and deliver software more quickly and reliably. It emphasizes continuous delivery, monitoring, and infrastructure automation.
fault_tolerance: “Fault tolerance” is the ability of a system to continue operating properly even when part of the system fails. This is achieved through design strategies like redundancy, failover mechanisms, and graceful degradation.
redundancy: “Redundancy” is the practice of having duplicate components or systems in place so that if one fails, another can take over. It is a key strategy for improving reliability and fault tolerance.
microservices: “Microservices” is an architectural approach where an application is broken into small, independent services that each handle a specific function and communicate over APIs. This allows for better scalability, flexibility, and independent deployment.
resiliency: “Resiliency” refers to a system’s ability to recover quickly from failures and continue operating. It focuses on minimizing downtime and maintaining service even under adverse conditions.
cicd_pipelines: “CI/CD pipelines” are automated workflows that handle the building, testing, and deployment of code changes. They enable teams to release software quickly and reliably by automating repetitive tasks and ensuring consistency.
infrastructure_as_code: “Infrastructure-as-code” is the practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure using code instead of manual processes. This allows for version control, automation, and reproducibility of environments.
segmentation: “Segmentation” is the process of dividing users or data into distinct groups based on shared characteristics such as behavior, preferences, or demographics. It is commonly used in personalization and targeting systems.
resource_usage_optimization: “Identifying resource usage for optimization” refers to analyzing how system resources like CPU, memory, and network are used in order to improve efficiency, reduce costs, and enhance performance.
real_time_processing: “Real-time processing” refers to handling data immediately as it is generated, allowing systems to respond instantly to events or inputs. This is critical for applications that require up-to-date information and fast reactions.
modern_data_platforms: “Modern data platforms” are systems designed to store, process, and analyze large volumes of data efficiently, often using cloud-based tools, distributed systems, and scalable storage solutions.
recommendation_systems: “Recommendation systems” are algorithms and systems that analyze user behavior and preferences to suggest relevant products, content, or actions. They are widely used to improve engagement and personalization in applications.
scalable_architecture: “Scalable architecture” refers to designing a system so it can handle increasing amounts of traffic, data, or usage without breaking down or requiring a complete rewrite. This involves structuring services, databases, and infrastructure in a way that allows you to add more resources (like servers or processing power), distribute workloads, and maintain performance and reliability as the system grows. In practice, this often includes patterns like microservices, load balancing, caching, and cloud-based, elastic infrastructure.
strong_system_designs: “Strong system designs” refers to creating software systems that are well-structured, reliable, and maintainable while meeting both current and future requirements. This includes making thoughtful decisions about architecture, data flow, APIs, performance, scalability, and failure handling. A strong design balances tradeoffs (like speed vs. complexity or flexibility vs. control), ensures components are loosely coupled and reusable, and anticipates real-world conditions such as high traffic, partial outages, and evolving product needs.
pragmatic_governance: “Pragmatic governance” means putting guidelines, standards, and oversight in place to keep systems consistent, secure, and maintainable—while still allowing teams to move quickly and make practical decisions. Instead of rigid, bureaucratic rules, it focuses on lightweight processes, clear ownership, and sensible guardrails that guide how code, content, and features are built and released. The goal is to balance control (quality, reliability, compliance) with speed and flexibility.
reliability: “Reliability” in software engineering means a system consistently works as expected under normal conditions and continues to function gracefully under stress or failure. This includes minimizing downtime, handling errors properly, ensuring data integrity, and designing for fault tolerance through techniques like redundancy, retries, monitoring, and automated recovery. A reliable system delivers correct results, responds within acceptable time limits, and maintains a predictable experience even as conditions change.
content_targeting: “Content targeting” is the practice of deciding which content to show to a specific user based on signals like behavior, preferences, location, device, or past interactions. It relies on rules, segmentation, or machine learning to dynamically select and deliver the most relevant content (such as banners, recommendations, or messaging) in real time. In a system like Chewy, this could mean showing different homepage modules or product recommendations depending on a customer's pet type, purchase history, or browsing patterns.
technical_leader: A “technical leader” is an engineer who guides the technical direction of a team or project by making sound architectural decisions, setting coding and design standards, and ensuring solutions are scalable, reliable, and maintainable. They influence how systems are built, review and improve others' work, mentor teammates, and collaborate with product and design to align technical solutions with business goals. They may not be a formal manager but are trusted to lead through expertise, judgment, and communication.
strong_engineering_judgment: “Strong engineering judgment” is the ability to make sound technical decisions by weighing tradeoffs such as performance, scalability, complexity, cost, and long-term maintainability. It involves choosing the right tools and approaches for a given problem, avoiding overengineering or shortcuts that create future issues, and adapting decisions based on real constraints like deadlines, team skill sets, and business goals. It also includes knowing when to challenge assumptions and when to move forward pragmatically.
design_principles: “Design principles” are fundamental guidelines that inform how software systems and components should be structured and built. They include concepts like separation of concerns, modularity, reusability, simplicity, and consistency, and they help engineers create systems that are easier to understand, maintain, and scale over time. Applying design principles means making intentional decisions about architecture, component boundaries, and data flow to reduce complexity and improve long-term quality.
operational_impact: “Operational impact” refers to how a technical decision or system affects the day-to-day running of a product in production. This includes factors like system reliability, performance, cost, monitoring, incident response, and ease of maintenance. Engineers consider operational impact to ensure their solutions don't just work when built, but continue to run smoothly, scale efficiently, and can be supported by the team over time.
hands_on_implementation: “Hands-on implementation” means actively writing code and building features yourself rather than only designing or overseeing work at a high level. It involves taking ownership of turning ideas, requirements, or system designs into working software—debugging issues, integrating APIs, optimizing performance, and ensuring the final solution meets technical and business needs.
solutions_that_scale: “Solutions that scale” are systems or features designed to handle growth in users, data, or traffic without degrading performance or requiring major rework. This involves building with flexibility and efficiency in mind—using patterns like horizontal scaling, caching, stateless services, and efficient data handling—so the system can expand smoothly as demand increases while maintaining reliability and performance.
measuring_experiences: When asking about how experiences are measured, you're trying to understand which metrics and systems Chewy uses to evaluate whether changes to their site actually improve customer outcomes. This includes quantitative metrics like conversion rate, click-through rate, engagement time, retention, and page performance, as well as experimentation frameworks like A/B testing to compare variations. It can also include qualitative signals such as customer feedback, support interactions, and usability insights. The goal is to learn how engineering work connects to measurable business impact and how success is defined and validated.
personalized_experience: A “personalized experience” is when a system adapts what it shows or how it behaves based on an individual user's data—such as their preferences, past behavior, location, or profile. Instead of a one-size-fits-all interface, the platform dynamically tailors content, recommendations, and messaging to better match each user's needs. In a product like Chewy, this could mean showing different products, promotions, or homepage content depending on a customer's pet type, purchase history, or browsing patterns.
groundbreaking_solutions: “Groundbreaking solutions to build high-performance, low-latency web and mobile experiences” means creating innovative technical approaches that significantly improve how fast and smoothly a website or app responds to users. This involves minimizing delays (latency), optimizing load times, and ensuring interactions feel instant across devices, often through techniques like efficient data fetching, caching strategies, edge delivery, and optimized rendering. The “groundbreaking” part implies not just following standard practices, but finding smarter or more advanced ways to improve speed, scalability, and overall user experience.
scalability: “Scalability” is the ability of a system to handle increasing amounts of work—such as more users, requests, or data—without a drop in performance or reliability. It involves designing software and infrastructure so resources can be added (like more servers or processing power), workloads can be distributed efficiently, and bottlenecks are minimized. Good scalability ensures the system continues to perform well as demand grows.
latency: “Latency” is the amount of time it takes for a system to respond to a request—such as how long it takes from when a user clicks something to when the result appears. It includes delays from network travel, server processing, and data fetching, and is a critical factor in performance, especially for real-time or interactive applications. Lower latency means faster responses and a smoother user experience.
performance_robustness: “Performance robustness” means a system can maintain stable, acceptable performance even under changing or stressful conditions—such as high traffic, slow dependencies, or partial failures. It's not just about being fast in ideal situations, but consistently delivering good response times and behavior despite spikes in load, network issues, or unexpected usage patterns. This often involves safeguards like graceful degradation, timeouts, retries, and efficient resource management.
cost_tradeoffs: “Cost trade-offs” refers to the decisions engineers make when balancing system performance, scalability, and reliability against the financial cost of infrastructure and resources. Improving speed or reliability often requires more servers, storage, or third-party services, which increases cost, so engineers must evaluate whether the benefit is worth the expense. This involves choosing efficient architectures, optimizing resource usage, and sometimes accepting slightly lower performance or complexity to keep systems economically sustainable.
cloud_environments: “Virtualized, elastic, cloud-based environments” refers to modern infrastructure where applications run on virtual machines or containers instead of physical servers, typically managed by cloud providers like AWS. “Virtualized” means resources (CPU, memory, storage) are abstracted and shared efficiently; “elastic” means those resources can automatically scale up or down based on demand; and “cloud-based” means everything is hosted remotely and accessed over the internet. This setup allows systems to dynamically adjust capacity, improve reliability, and optimize cost.
react_expertise: “Expertise in React” means having a deep understanding of how to build efficient, scalable, and maintainable user interfaces using React's core concepts—such as components, hooks, state management, and rendering behavior. It includes knowing how to structure applications, manage data flow (including tools like GraphQL or query libraries), optimize performance (memoization, avoiding unnecessary re-renders), and integrate with broader systems like APIs, design systems, and testing frameworks. It also involves making good architectural decisions and debugging complex UI issues.
graphql_expertise: “Expertise in GraphQL” means understanding how to design, query, and optimize APIs using GraphQL effectively. This includes structuring schemas (types, queries, mutations), handling relationships between data, and ensuring efficient data fetching by avoiding over-fetching or under-fetching. It also involves working with resolvers, caching strategies, pagination, error handling, and performance considerations, as well as integrating GraphQL with frontend frameworks like React and tools like Apollo or Relay.
java_kotlin_expertise: “Expertise in Java (Kotlin)” means being able to design, build, and maintain robust backend systems using these languages, with a strong grasp of object-oriented programming, concurrency, and performance. It includes working with common frameworks (like Spring), building APIs and services, handling data persistence, managing multithreading, and ensuring systems are scalable and reliable. It also involves writing clean, maintainable code, applying design patterns appropriately, and understanding how these services operate in distributed, cloud-based environments.
cloud_native_stack: A “cloud-native tech stack” refers to building and running applications specifically designed for cloud environments rather than traditional on-premise servers. This includes using services and patterns like containers (e.g., Docker), orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes), microservices, managed databases, and scalable infrastructure from providers like AWS. Cloud-native systems are designed to be resilient, automatically scalable, and easy to deploy and update, often using CI/CD pipelines and infrastructure-as-code.
service_oriented_architecture: “Service-oriented architecture” (SOA) is a way of designing systems where functionality is split into separate, independent services that communicate with each other over a network (often via APIs). Each service handles a specific responsibility—such as user accounts, payments, or content—and can be developed, deployed, and scaled independently. This approach improves flexibility, scalability, and fault isolation compared to a single monolithic system.
critical_business_needs: “Critical business needs implemented by the applied principles from emerging technologies” means using new and evolving technologies—like AI, cloud-native systems, or modern data platforms—in a practical, thoughtful way to solve important problems that directly impact the business. It's not about using new tech for its own sake, but understanding its core principles (e.g., scalability, automation, real-time processing) and applying them to improve things like customer experience, operational efficiency, or revenue growth.
ecommerce_scale: “Ecommerce products at ecommerce scale” refers to systems built to support very high volumes of users, transactions, and data typical of large online retailers. This includes handling millions of visitors, large product catalogs, real-time inventory updates, payments, and personalized experiences—all while maintaining fast performance, high availability, and reliability. It requires scalable architecture, efficient data systems, and robust integrations across services like search, recommendations, and order processing.
kubernetes: Kubernetes is an open-source system used to manage, deploy, and scale containerized applications across clusters of machines. It automates tasks like scheduling containers, handling failures, load balancing, rolling updates, and scaling resources up or down based on demand. In modern cloud-native systems, Kubernetes helps ensure applications remain highly available, resilient, and efficiently utilize infrastructure.
dynamodb: DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL database designed for high performance and scalability, commonly used in cloud-native applications. It stores data in a flexible key-value or document format and is optimized for fast read/write operations at large scale without requiring traditional relational schemas. “DynamoDB or related database technologies” refers to experience with similar distributed, non-relational databases (like Cassandra or MongoDB) that prioritize scalability, availability, and low latency over complex joins and strict relational structure.
automated_testing_tools: Automated testing tools like Cypress and Playwright are used to programmatically test web applications by simulating real user interactions—such as clicking buttons, filling forms, and navigating pages. These tools help ensure that features work correctly across different browsers and scenarios, catch regressions early, and support continuous integration workflows by running tests automatically whenever code changes are made.
